critic_raw:
Overall, this is a carefully constructed evaluation design. The temporal split is correct, preprocessing discipline is solid, and the scope is appropriately bounded. I found a small number of genuine issues worth raising, none of which are catastrophic but a couple of which could meaningfully distort conclusions.

**Issue 1: Test-set peeking invalidates holdout guarantees**

The specific claim: Section 8 describes a 'confirmatory pass' on the test set after hyperparameter selection on validation, where the selected configuration is verified to 'maintain its advantage' on the test window before final retraining. This is framed as a guard against validation-period overfit.

Why this is wrong: This is a form of conditional model selection conditioned on test-set performance. Even a single decision — 'does this configuration hold up on months 31–36?' — uses the test set as a decision surface. If the answer had been 'no, it doesn't hold up,' the implied next action is to revise the configuration. The fact that no additional search is performed after this step does not eliminate the contamination: the decision to proceed was itself informed by test labels. The test set is no longer a strictly held-out measurement of generalization; it is now a gate. Bootstrap confidence intervals computed on the same window are valid only under the assumption that the test set was untouched prior to final evaluation, which is now violated.

What would constitute evidence one way or the other: If the confirmatory pass never caused a revision to the selected configuration across any run of this experiment (i.e., the answer was always 'yes, it holds up'), the contamination is minimal in practice, though still present in principle. The design should either (a) eliminate the confirmatory pass and accept that validation-period overfit is a known limitation, or (b) reserve a true fourth holdout window (months 31–33 for confirmation, months 34–36 for final evaluation). As written, the confidence intervals reported on months 31–36 are optimistic to an unknown degree.

**Issue 2: Label freeze at month 18 creates asymmetric training signal for the two halves of the training set**

The specific claim: Freezing labels at month 18 prevents label drift contaminating the evaluation. This is treated as a clean solution.

Why this might be wrong: If team ownership genuinely shifts after month 18, tickets from months 19–24 (still in the training set) will be labeled under the frozen schema but their text will reflect the new team's actual scope. The model trains on these tickets as if routing is consistent, when in fact the routing signal for months 19–24 may be noisier or inverted relative to the frozen label. The label freeze is a correct decision for the test window, but it does not sanitize the late-training-window data — it may actively corrupt it if team ownership shifted during that window. This is not a distributional shift problem; it is a label noise problem localized to the period between the shift and the freeze.

What would constitute evidence one way or the other: Stratifying training-set accuracy (not just test-set accuracy) by month and looking for a degradation or reversal in months 19–24 for affected queues would surface this. If the model's training-set loss is anomalously high for those months in specific queues, that is a signal of label-text inconsistency. The design should document when team ownership changes actually occurred relative to month 18.

**Issue 3: Reply history in transformer inputs introduces a form of leakage that is absent from the TF-IDF baseline by design, not by feature parity**

The specific claim: Both models receive the same concatenated text (subject + body + reply history), so any transformer advantage reflects architecture, not feature access.

Why this is wrong: Reply history is partially a function of the routing decision that already occurred. An agent who received a misrouted ticket may write a reply that explicitly mentions it should go elsewhere, or a correctly routed agent may reference domain-specific terminology that encodes the queue label. In a deployed system, the routing decision must be made before any agent reply exists. If reply history is included, the evaluation is measuring performance on a feature that is unavailable at inference time. This affects both models equally in terms of feature access, but it inflates both models' measured accuracy relative to their operational accuracy. The comparison between transformer and TF-IDF remains internally valid, but neither number corresponds to deployable performance.

What would constitute evidence one way or the other: Running both models with and without reply history and comparing the delta. If reply history provides a large accuracy boost, the headline numbers are operationally misleading. The design claims the experiment tests 'genuine generalization to distribution shift' and supports a 'faster first-response time' claim — both of which require that the features used at eval time match features available at routing time.

**Issue 4: Retraining on months 1–30 after hyperparameter selection means the final deployed model was never directly evaluated**

The specific claim: The final model is retrained on months 1–30 using locked hyperparameters, and metrics are reported on the test window.

Why this is wrong: The test set metrics are computed on a model trained on months 1–24, not the final retrained model (months 1–30). The retrained model is what would be deployed, but its performance on months 31–36 is never directly measured — the reported numbers come from the month-1–24-trained version. If the validation set (months 25–30) introduces distributional characteristics that improve or harm the retrained model, the reported test metrics are a proxy for, not a measurement of, the deployed model's behavior. This is a common but underappreciated gap in experimental designs that retrain after selection.

What would constitute evidence one way or the other: Reporting both the selected-configuration model (trained on months 1–24) and the retrained model (trained on months 1–30) on the test set would quantify this gap. In practice the difference is often small, but for a temporal distribution shift experiment it is not guaranteed to be — adding six months of data could change feature importance significantly in a system experiencing product evolution.

**Non-issues worth briefly noting:**
The temporal split rationale is correct and well-justified. The equal tuning effort for both models is a genuine strength. The FRT secondary metric is operationally meaningful. The customer-memorization post-hoc analysis is a good confound check. The scope limitations in Section 10 are appropriately modest.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Issue 1: Test-set peeking invalidates holdout guarantees

**Critic's claim:** The 'confirmatory pass' in Section 8 constitutes conditional model selection on the test set. Even a single binary decision ('does the configuration hold up?') uses the test set as a decision surface, contaminating the holdout and making bootstrap confidence intervals optimistic to an unknown degree.

**Assessment:** This is a valid critique. The critic is correct in the strict sense. A binary gate — 'proceed if the configuration holds, revise if not' — is still a decision conditioned on test-set outcomes. The framing in Section 8 tries to have it both ways: it wants the rigor of a held-out test window and the safety of a sanity check against validation-period overfit. These goals are in tension, and the design resolves the tension incorrectly.

The defense I can partially mount is that the contamination is bounded in practice: if the confirmatory pass was designed to catch only gross failures (e.g., a configuration that performs at chance on months 31–36 due to a label mapping error) rather than to fine-tune selection, the effective degrees of freedom consumed are very small. But this is an implementation detail not stated in the design, and the critic is right that as written the procedure is not clean. The confidence intervals cannot be asserted as valid under strict holdout assumptions.

**Concession:** Full concession. The confirmatory pass as described contaminates the test set. The two remedies the critic proposes — eliminate the pass entirely, or reserve months 31–33 for confirmation and 34–36 for final evaluation — are both better than the current design. The second option is preferable given the stated goal of guarding against validation-period overfit.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether in practice the confirmatory pass ever caused a revision. If the experiment has already been run and the pass never triggered a revision, the contamination is minimal in practice. But the design as written does not guarantee this, and it should be corrected prospectively.

---

### Issue 2: Label freeze at month 18 creates asymmetric training signal for months 19–24

**Critic's claim:** Freezing labels at month 18 correctly protects the test window from label drift, but it does not sanitize training tickets from months 19–24. If team ownership shifted before month 18 (or the freeze date itself is approximate), tickets from months 19–24 may have text reflecting new routing reality while carrying frozen labels that no longer match — creating label noise in the late training window rather than preventing it.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and underappreciated concern. The design acknowledges qualitatively that 'team ownership and product coverage may drift over the 36-month window' but treats the freeze as a solution. The critic correctly identifies that the freeze is a partial solution: it protects the evaluation labels from drift but does not protect late-training-window examples from the same drift.

The partial defense is that label noise in months 19–24 would likely harm the model's training-set fit on those examples, which would show up as elevated training or validation loss for affected queues in that window. This is diagnosable if the team stratifies training-set performance by month, which the design does not currently specify. The critic's proposed diagnostic — stratifying training-set accuracy by month and looking for anomalous loss in months 19–24 for affected queues — is exactly right and should be added to the experimental protocol.

However, I also want to note a mild overstatement in the critique: label noise in the late training window harms both models equally (since both train on the same labels), so it does not directionally bias the transformer vs. baseline comparison. It does, however, reduce the overall accuracy of both models and could cause the experiment to underestimate the ceiling of performance achievable with clean labels.

**Concession:** Partial concession. The design should document when team ownership changes occurred relative to month 18 and should add the month-stratified training diagnostic. The label freeze is necessary but not sufficient. The direction of bias in the transformer vs. baseline comparison is not clearly affected, but the absolute accuracy numbers may be depressed for both models if late-training data is noisy.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether team ownership actually shifted during months 19–24. If the company's historical records show the freeze date coincides with the actual last ownership change, this issue is moot. The design should verify this empirically.

---

### Issue 3: Reply history is unavailable at inference time, inflating both models' measured accuracy

**Critic's claim:** Agent reply history is included as a feature in both models. In a deployed routing system, routing must occur before any agent reply exists. Reply history thus encodes the routing decision already made and is unavailable at true inference time. Both models benefit equally, so the internal comparison is valid, but neither model's measured accuracy corresponds to deployable performance. The claims about 'faster first-response time' and 'genuine generalization to distribution shift' are undermined.

**Assessment:** This is the most operationally significant critique. The critic is correct. If reply history is agent replies written after the ticket was routed, it is temporally posterior to the routing event and cannot be used as an input to a routing classifier deployed on incoming tickets. The design's claim that 'any transformer advantage is not simply due to access to longer context or metadata; both models have equal feature access' is true for the internal comparison but does not rescue the external validity problem.

I want to explore whether there is any version of this that is defensible. If 'reply history' is defined to include only the customer's own follow-up messages (not agent replies), and if some support workflows allow routing to be re-evaluated after a customer follow-up, then there may be a legitimate use case. But the design does not make this distinction — it says 'subject + body + reply history' is concatenated, and in support ticketing systems 'reply history' standardly includes agent responses.

Additionally, the critic is right that the FRT secondary metric is undermined: if the model uses reply history to achieve high routing accuracy, but in deployment the model only sees the original ticket text, the FRT gains attributed to the model in this experiment would not materialize in production.

**Concession:** Full concession on the operational validity concern. The design must specify whether reply history is (a) customer follow-up text only, available at initial routing time, (b) agent replies, unavailable at initial routing time, or (c) a mix. If (b) or (c), the experiment should be run with and without reply history to isolate the inflation. The headline accuracy and FRT numbers should be qualified accordingly.

**What remains uncertain:** The magnitude of the inflation. If reply history provides modest lift (e.g., 1–2% accuracy), the numbers are operationally misleading but not dramatically so. If it provides large lift (e.g., 10–15%), the entire experiment's practical claims are called into question. The critic's proposed ablation is necessary.

---

### Issue 4: The final retrained model (months 1–30) is never directly evaluated

**Critic's claim:** Test-set metrics are computed on the model trained on months 1–24. The final model actually deployed is retrained on months 1–30. The deployed model's performance on months 31–36 is never directly measured. In a temporal distribution shift experiment, adding six months of validation data to training could materially change the model.

**Assessment:** This is a valid methodological observation, but its severity depends on context. This pattern — select hyperparameters on validation, retrain on train+validation, evaluate on test — is standard practice and is not in itself a flaw. The implicit assumption is that the model trained on months 1–24 and evaluated on months 31–36 is a conservative proxy for the model trained on months 1–30 and evaluated on months 31–36, because the latter has seen more recent data.

The critic is right that this assumption is not guaranteed to hold in temporal distribution shift settings. The retrained model has seen months 25–30, which are closer in time to months 31–36, and may have learned more recent routing patterns. This could make the retrained model better or worse on months 31–36 depending on whether months 25–30 represent a drift that continues into months 31–36 or a transient perturbation.

The gap is real but commonly accepted in practice, and the critic acknowledges 'the difference is often small.' The design could close this gap by reporting both the selection-configuration model and the retrained model on the test set, as the critic suggests. This is a relatively low-cost addition to the protocol.

**Concession:** Partial concession. The gap exists and is worth quantifying. In a temporal distribution shift experiment specifically, the assumption that the selection-model performance is a valid proxy for retrained-model performance is weaker than in a static distribution setting. The design should report both to quantify the gap. This is a known limitation of the standard retrain-after-selection protocol, not a design error unique to this experiment, but it should be acknowledged explicitly.

**What remains uncertain:** The magnitude of the gap. If months 25–30 represent stable routing patterns, the gap is likely small. If they represent a period of reorganization, the retrained model may perform meaningfully differently.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Reviewing my Pass 1 concessions:

- **Issue 1:** Full concession. Test-set contamination via confirmatory pass. This is a genuine methodological flaw affecting the validity of the confidence intervals.
- **Issue 2:** Partial concession. Label noise in months 19–24 is a real risk; the design's label freeze is necessary but not sufficient. Does not directionally bias the comparison but may depress absolute accuracy.
- **Issue 3:** Full concession. Reply history leakage is the most operationally significant flaw. It inflates both models' accuracy and undermines the FRT and deployment claims.
- **Issue 4:** Partial concession. The retrained-model evaluation gap is a known limitation that is more consequential in temporal shift settings than static ones.

I have made two full concessions (Issues 1 and 3) on points that affect the validity of the core claims: Issue 1 undermines the statistical guarantees on the reported metrics, and Issue 3 undermines the operational relevance of the accuracy and FRT claims. These are not cosmetic issues. The methodology would need to (a) eliminate or restructure the confirmatory pass, (b) clarify and likely ablate reply history as a feature, (c) add month-stratified diagnostics for label noise, and (d) report both evaluation configurations for the retrain gap — before the reported numbers can be taken at face value for the stated claims.

The methodology is recoverable — none of the issues require abandoning the design — but as written it has critical gaps. The verdict must reflect that these are not merely theoretical concerns but issues that affect the interpretability and operational validity of the primary claims.
all_issues_raised: The 'confirmatory pass' on the test set in Section 8 constitutes conditional model selection on held-out data, invalidating the holdout guarantee regardless of whether additional search follows.
Freezing labels at month 18 does not sanitize training tickets from months 19–24, which may contain label-text inconsistencies if team ownership shifted before the freeze date.

Reply history is included as a training and evaluation feature but is unavailable at inference time in a deployed routing system, inflating both models' measured accuracy relative to operational accuracy.

The final deployed model (retrained on months 1–30) is never directly evaluated on the test set; reported metrics come from the month-1–24-trained model, creating an unquantified gap between evaluation and deployment.
all_issues_adjudicated: The confirmatory pass on the test set in Section 8 constitutes conditional model selection on held-out data, contaminating the holdout and invalidating the bootstrap confidence intervals as stated; the defender fully conceded this.

Reply history is temporally posterior to the routing decision and unavailable at true inference time, inflating both models' measured accuracy and undermining the FRT and deployment claims; the defender fully conceded this.

Label freeze at month 18 does not sanitize training tickets from months 19–24, which may carry label-text inconsistencies if team ownership shifted before the freeze; the defender partially conceded this and noted the design lacks the diagnostic to detect it.

The final retrained model (months 1–30) is never directly evaluated on the test set; reported metrics come from the month-1–24-trained model, creating an unquantified gap that is more consequential in temporal distribution shift settings; the defender partially conceded this.
